sections.heading: The national pattern turns sharply after one child
sections.body: Nationally, 57.3 percent of currently married women wanted no more children or were sterilized in 2025, almost unchanged from 57.6 percent in 2022. The larger shift is across family size: the share rises from 26.1 percent among women with one living child to 63.3 percent among women with two living children. By three living children, 78.4 percent wanted no more children, and the level stayed above 80 percent for women with four or more. The 2025 pattern suggests that the clearest transition happens between one and two children, even though the national total barely moved.

sections.heading: Urban and rural totals are nearly identical, but timing differs
sections.body: The overall urban-rural difference was small in 2025, with 57.5 percent of urban women and 57.1 percent of rural women wanting no more children or being sterilized. Urban women were more likely than rural women to want no more children at one child, 29.1 percent versus 22.2 percent, and at two children, 65.2 percent versus 61.0 percent. The urban share also reached 91.8 percent among women with six or more living children, compared with 87.7 percent in rural areas. This is less a story of different overall levels than of urban women reaching a high desire to stop slightly earlier in the family-size sequence.

sections.heading: Education differences mostly reflect where women are in the family-size sequence
sections.body: Total desire to stop childbearing was highest among women with Grades 1-6 education at 68.1 percent and lowest among women with Grades 11-12 at 23.1 percent. That gap narrows once women have two living children: 59.4 percent of women with Grades 1-6 and 48.8 percent of women with Grades 11-12 wanted no more children. Among women with college education, the two-child level was 65.1 percent, close to the national two-child level of 63.3 percent. The table therefore shows that the education pattern is much less pronounced once the comparison is made within the same family-size group.

sections.heading: Wealth differences are modest at the total level, but sharper at larger family sizes
sections.body: Across wealth quintiles, total desire to stop childbearing ranged from 55.4 percent in the highest quintile to 59.4 percent in the second quintile in 2025. The spread is small at the national total level, but it widens at some higher parities. Among women with four living children, 94.2 percent in the highest quintile wanted no more children, compared with 79.7 percent in the lowest quintile. The data suggest that wealth differences are not mainly about whether women ever want to stop, but about how strongly that preference appears once families are already larger.

sections.heading: BARMM remains the lowest region, even after large gains
sections.body: BARMM had the lowest total desire to limit childbearing in 2025 at 29.7 percent, far below the national level of 57.3 percent. It still rose from 22.6 percent in 2022, and the gains appear across several family sizes, including 26.4 percent at two children and 58.8 percent at six or more children in 2025. Even at three living children, only 34.9 percent of currently married women in BARMM wanted no more children, compared with 78.4 percent nationally. The region is therefore not just low overall; its desire to stop rises later and remains lower at nearly every family size.

sections.heading: Zamboanga Peninsula fell sharply from its 2022 level
sections.body: Zamboanga Peninsula¹ had one of the clearest declines in the regional table, with the total falling from 58.8 percent in 2022 to 42.7 percent in 2025. The decline is especially visible at two and three living children, dropping from 58.3 to 44.6 percent at two children and from 73.6 to 55.1 percent at three children. The region also remained low at higher family sizes, including 62.4 percent at four children and 68.4 percent at six or more. This makes the region stand out not only because the total is low, but because the desire to stop is weaker across much of the family-size ladder.

sections.heading: Several high-level regions now cross 60 percent overall
sections.body: CAR, MIMAROPA, and Bicol were among the highest regions in 2025, at 63.7, 63.2, and 62.3 percent, respectively. CAR is especially notable because it rose from 55.3 percent in 2022 and reached 68.4 percent by two living children and 87.2 percent by three. MIMAROPA also showed a high total with a relatively early rise, reaching 65.8 percent at two children and 77.7 percent at three. These regions show how high overall levels are often built not only from very large families, but from a strong shift toward stopping by the second or third child.

sections.heading: Some regions changed more than the national total suggests
sections.body: The national total was nearly flat, but regional movement was much less stable. Bicol fell from 70.1 percent in 2022 to 62.3 percent in 2025, while Western Visayas declined from 65.0 to 58.5 percent and Zamboanga Peninsula¹ declined from 58.8 to 42.7 percent. In the other direction, BARMM rose from 22.6 to 29.7 percent, CAR rose from 55.3 to 63.7 percent, and Northern Mindanao rose from 54.2 to 59.5 percent. The flat national trend therefore masks a set of regional shifts moving in different directions.

takeaways: National desire to limit childbearing was almost unchanged from 2022, but the sharpest transition remains between one and two living children.
BARMM and Zamboanga Peninsula¹ stand out as low regional outliers, while CAR, MIMAROPA, and Bicol are among the highest regions overall.
Urban-rural and wealth differences are modest in total, but family size reveals stronger contrasts in when the desire to stop childbearing becomes widespread.
footnotes: ¹ Includes the province of Sulu in 2025.
